data.title: GE Aerospace Raises Guidance After 2Q Beat
data.description:
By Katherine Hamilton

GE Aerospace raised its full-year earnings outlook after beating expectations on its top and bottom lines in the second quarter.

The aircraft engine maker on Thursday posted a profit of $2.03 billion, or $1.87 a share, in the quarter ended June 30, compared with $1.20 billion, or $1.15 a share, a year earlier.

Stripping out certain one-time items, adjusted per-share earnings were $1.66, ahead of the $1.43 forecast by analysts, according to FactSet.

Revenue rose 21% to $11.02 billion. Analysts surveyed by FactSet had forecast revenue of $9.59 billion.

GE raised its 2025 and 2028 outlook. It now anticipates adjusted earnings per share to be $5.60 to $5.80 this year, up from prior guidance of $5.10 to $5.45. It increased its three-year projections for adjusted revenue growth and operating profit, and set an adjusted earnings goalpost of $8.40 a share in 2028.

Chief Executive and chairman H. Lawrence Culp cited higher revenue, earnings and cash growth expectations as fueling the outlook boost.

Orders in its commercial engines and services business increased 28% to $11.69 billion. Defense and propulsion technologies orders climbed 24% to $2.90 billion.

Write to Katherine Hamilton at katherine.hamilton@wsj.com

data.title: Diesel cash diffs back near 1.5-year high; regrade at widest since Jan
data.description:
Asia's middle distillates markets were without deals again on the trading window, with spot liquidity remaining thin and traders eyeing further August spot refiner offers.

The east-west price spread narrowed further to discounts of around $31 per metric ton, as Asian August paper markets continued to firm amid expectation of more stock draws for the month given limited swing supply arrivals.

So far in July, there have been no shipments of diesel loaded from Asia-Pacific to northwest Europe, according to shipping data and multiple trade sources.

However, jet fuel exports on the east-west trade route are slated to remain high, continuing the trend from June, given weaker northeast Asian prices, supplies and slightly better arbitrage margins compared to diesel.

Refining margins (GO10SGCKMc1) gained for a second straight session to around $21 a barrel, back to nearly one-month high levels.

On the trading window, deals were again scant but the 10ppm sulphur gasoil cash differentials (GO10-SIN-DIF) remained supported, heading back to nearly 1.5-year highs of around $1.45 a barrel given the firm August-September timespread.

Regrade (JETREG10SGMc1) discount widened to more than $2.2 a barrel, the largest discount since early January this year as a reflection of the firmer gasoil paper markets levels.

SINGAPORE CASH DEALS

- No deals for both fuels

INVENTORIES

- U.S. crude oil stockpiles fell last week as exports rose, while gasoline and distillate inventories rose, prompting some concerns about fuel demand, the Energy Information Administration said on Wednesday.

- Singapore's middle distillates inventories declined for the fourth straight week amid a slight decline in net diesel exports and increase in jet fuel net exports, official data showed on Thursday.

REFINERY NEWS

- Phillips 66 PSX.N has partially shut its 258,500 barrel-per-day Bayway refinery in Linden, New Jersey, after rainstorms in the region caused a power outage at the plant, three sources familiar with the matter said.

NEWS

- India is confident of meeting its oil needs from alternative sources if Russian supplies are hit by secondary sanctions, oil minister Hardeep Singh Puri said on Thursday.

- The average Russian oil price calculated in roubles has stayed below the federal budget's target for 2025, Reuters calculations showed on Wednesday, adding pressure to the budget that already faces a rising deficit.

- Oil prices edged up on Thursday on signs of easing trade tensions, stronger than expected economic data from the world's top oil consumers and renewed risks in the Middle East.

data.title: Harmony Gold Mining Initiated at Market Perform by BMO Capital
Fifth Third, Citizens Post Steady 2Q EPS Growth
data.description:
By Rob Curran

Two major regional banks echoed their larger nationwide peers' theme of steady growth in second-quarter earnings updates.

Fifth Third Bancorp's net income rose 4.5% in the second quarter. The Cincinnati bank holding company posted a net profit of $628 million, or 88 cents a share, up from $601 million, or 81 cents a share, a year earlier. Earnings were weighed down by 2 cents a share in one-off charges. Analysts surveyed by FactSet had forecast earnings of 87 cents a share, according to FactSet.

The lender booked a provision for credit losses of $173 million, down slightly from first-quarter levels but up substantially from a year earlier. The charge-off ratio, another key measure of credit quality, fell slightly to 0.45%.

Fifth Third's net interest income rose 7.7% to $1.50 billion, surpassing the average analyst estimate of $1.48 billion, as per FactSet.

Similarly, Citizens Financial Group's second-quarter net income rose 6.9%.

The Providence, R.I., bank posted adjusted earnings of $436 million, or 92 cents a share, up from $408 million or 78 cents a share, a year earlier.

Citizens also posted adjusted earnings, reflecting any one-off items, of 92 cents a share, topping the mean analyst estimate of 88 cents a share.

Net interest income rose 1.9% to $1.44 billion, shy of the average Wall Street target of $1.48 billion.

Revenue rose 3.8% to $2.04 billion.

In a good sign for credit quality, Citizens' net charge-offs fell slightly on a sequential quarterly basis to 48 basis points.

"We are well-positioned to have a strong second half of the year and to sustain that momentum into the medium-term," said Chairman and Chief Executive Bruce Van Saun, in a statement. The bank remains comfortable with its January growth projections for the year.

JPMorgan Chase and Bank of America, the largest U.S. banks by assets under management, also logged steady growth in the second quarter, and indicated the economy was chugging along.

Wells Fargo caught investors off guard, however, when the San Francisco bank forecast a slowdown in net interest income.

One strategist said U.S. banks are likely to face headwinds in the balance of the year.

"While the first half of 2025 looks promising for U.S. banks, with continued margin expansion and stable loan growth, the second half presents potential challenges," said Nathan Stovall, a director of financial institution research at S&P Global Market Intelligence, in e-mailed commentary on the sector.

"The lingering effects of tariffs are expected to dampen economic growth and weigh on the consumer, leading to increased delinquencies."

Write to Rob Curran at rob.curran@dowjones.com

data.title: BMO Notes Canada's Moves to Protect Its Steel Industry
data.description:
Prime Minister Mark Carney announced on Wednesday that Canada will put 50% tariffs on non-CUSMA steel imports above last year's levels, in a renewed effort to support the sector, noted Bank of Montreal (BMO).

Even before the United States' tariffs on steel and aluminum were doubled to 50% in early June, there were already some pretty serious signs of stress in the Canadian industry, said the bank.

Exports to the U.S. of these metals and related products fell heavily in April and May — down 25% from average 2024 levels, and down 33% from March.

For perspective, these exports were a bit above $34 billion last year, or about 1.1% of gross domestic product, pointed out BMO. Imports from the U.S. of said goods were roughly half as large, and they too have been subject to tariffs, slicing into those volumes as well.

Along with autos and aluminum, the steel industry faces the greatest challenge in the trade battle with the U.S., and the force of Canada's announcement reflects that reality, stated the bank.

Lockton Re: ‘Substantial capacity growth and evolution’ in cyber reinsurance this year
data.description:
(The Insurer) - The key themes for the cyber reinsurance market in the first half of 2025 were increased capacity and treaty product innovation, together with continued cyber incidents, according to Lockton Re.

In a cyber reinsurance update, Lockton Re said: “Overall, the cyber reinsurance market has experienced substantial capacity growth and evolution in 2025 thus far. In the absence of a large loss event, this appears to be the position for the foreseeable future.”

Lockton Re highlighted a continued increase in reinsurance capacity this year following the entry of new participants in the latter half of 2024.

“Supply is outstripping demand in the short term,” the report said. “Some new entities have fresh balance sheets to deploy capital, while others are established markets, new to the cyber class of business. This has contributed approximately $250 million in additional capacity, the majority of which is non-proportional.”

This has allowed cedants to be more demanding either about financial security or economic or contractual terms.

”Incumbent markets can be replaced if there is misalignment on pricing or strategy. Another influence is that more established cedants have shown increased comfort of their attritional loss ratio and consequently are retaining increased net positions or non-renewing layers which have previously not demonstrated value,” the report said.

Reinsurers remain concerned about systemic risk, while concentration around key technology vendors is “firmly on reinsurers’ radar”, Lockton Re said.

The broker continued that cyber reinsurers remain profitable but there are signs of pressure amid a softening market. It said that margins are tightening as a result of falling rates, broader coverage and increasingly aggressive terms.

Non-proportional structures, particularly higher-layer excess of loss, remain the most profitable while ceding commissions have continued to rise for proportional treaties.

“Overall, the market is seeing a growing divergence in reinsurer strategy between margin protection and volume-driven growth, amidst rising volatility and increased buyer leverage,” the report said.

Treaty product structures continue to evolve, with increased interest in event-based products and hybrid structures, including triggers tied to specific cyber perils.

“However, given the perceived basis risk and oversupply, the aggregate stop loss structure has remained popular, with some looking to refine ASL terms to try and realise more value,” the report said.

There was continued softening on non-proportional reinsurance, with reinsurer appetite growing despite rate reductions.

“Larger, more mature cedants with strong portfolio performance leveraged this environment to reduce attachment points. Cedant risk tolerance has generally increased, which has been largely influenced by reduced uncertainty in attritional underwriting performance,” the report said.

In addition, some cedants focused on protecting against catastrophic cyber events and systemic exposures rather than large shock losses.

FIRST-TIME BUYER TAKE-UP RATE RISING

Lockton Re noted that the direct cyber insurance market continued to soften, with average premium rates declining by 5% to 15% depending on sector, geography and loss history.

The take-up rate among first-time cyber insurance buyers has been rising, especially outside the U.S., driven by greater awareness of the risks.

“Additionally, coverage expanded with some reduced deductibles and broader contingent business interruption, extending cover to include non-IT providers. Other coverage expansion areas include reputational harm extensions and regulatory response modules,” the report said.

The broker added that some markets are now underwriting higher-risk sectors that have historically struggled to secure cover, such as public healthcare.

REINSURERS SUPPORTING LOCAL DISTRIBUTION MODELS

In addition, interest in regions such as Latin America, Southeast Asia and the Middle East grew last year, and there has been an increase in exploratory partnerships, local MGA development and demand for capacity to support cyber products.

“Reinsurers have shown greater willingness to support localised distribution models, viewing them as a way to diversify portfolios and capture growth,” the report said. “Capacity is deployed cautiously though, with modest limits exposed, often on a quota share basis whilst pricing (and local loss experience) is relatively uncertain.”

Reinsurers are closely monitoring underwriting controls and data quality, Lockton Re added.

Lockton Re also highlighted a series of significant cyber ransomware attacks in the past six months, including on major retailers in the UK, U.S. education service providers and insurance companies, as well as South Korean telecoms.

“Whilst some affected firms purchased cyber insurance policies (which are expected to payout millions in claims), others did not,” the report said. “The publicity of uninsured losses has generated renewed scrutiny and interest from those companies without cover and underinsured peers. This will continue to drive demand for first-time buyers.”

In response to the attacks, reinsurers have focused continued attention on supply chain resilience, cyber hygiene and coverage clarity.

data.title: Brazil lawmakers gut environmental permitting ahead of COP30 summit
data.description:
Brazil's Congress passed legislation on Thursday loosening environmental licensing, a potential blow to President Luiz Inacio Lula da Silva as he seeks to burnish his country's green credentials before hosting the United Nations global climate summit, COP30, this year.

The bill, which was approved by the lower house early on Thursday after passing the Senate in May, allows projects considered to have a small or mid-sized impact, such as dams and basic sanitation infrastructure, to be built without environmental permits.

Lula can still veto parts or the whole bill, sending it back to Congress, which could override his veto.

data.title: DP World halves Romania-Turkey shipment times as it bolsters Black Sea assets
data.description:
Dubai-owned ports and logistics company DP World has cut shipping times between Romania and Turkey by half, a senior executive said, as it expands its Black Sea operations at the European Union's eastern border.

DP World more than doubled its container shipping capacity at Romania's largest Black Sea port of Constanta last year, with the addition of a roll-on, roll-off (Ro-Ro) terminal, and a logistics hub in western Romania.

It has this year added an integrated European logistics service out of Romania offering road, sea, air and rail services, initially for clients in neighbouring Bulgaria, Moldova, Serbia and Turkey.

"We have made huge strides in optimising maritime trade between Turkey and Constanta, improving the transit time by sea by 50%," DP World Eastern Europe Vice President Kris Adams told Reuters.

"Istanbul-to-Bucharest shipments have been cut to under 30 hours, creating a viable alternative to road transport."

A new scanner at DP World's Constanta Ro-Ro terminal has reduced truck inspection times from 5-7 hours to 2 minutes, he added.

The upgrades cost a total of 130 million euros, but Adams said more would follow as there is growth potential in automotive, e-commerce and renewable energy cargo.

"We're looking at ultimately investing over 200 million euros, there will be more in staff, in a digital platform and ... we have an ambition to operate contract logistics, so that means that we will at some stage be operating warehouses but it is too early to comment."

Investment in infrastructure and exports of Ukrainian grain through Constanta in the wake of Russia's 2022 invasion helped boost operations at the port, which is a gateway to Central Asia and provides access to the Danube river and Western Europe.

Its importance has also grown as companies relocate operations to Eastern Europe's low-cost manufacturing hubs to shorten supply chains.

Adams said the company's investment plans would continue regardless of pending tax hikes by Romania's broad coalition government, although he noted the rises could dampen trade.
